---
# ! Render via `targets::tar_make()` (the `render_manuscript` target in
# ! _targets.R), not `quarto render` directly - see manuscript.qmd.
title: "How a .qmd becomes html/docx/pdf: the compute → filter → convert pipeline"
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
library(here)
here::i_am("manuscript/supplementary/rendering-pipeline.qmd")
```

{{< include _back-to-manuscript.qmd >}}

::: {.callout-tip}
# The Short Version
- One `.qmd` is executed **once** by knitr, producing a single intermediate
  markdown document with computed output already substituted in — R runs
  once per render, not once per output format.
- That intermediate markdown then goes through Quarto's Lua filters
  (cross-reference numbering, this project's `flextable-qmd` filter), and
  only *then* to pandoc, which has a separate writer per output format.
- docx and typst aren't "pandoc understands Word/PDF directly" — docx is
  pandoc writing OOXML text with **named style references**, and typst is
  pandoc writing Typst *markup*, which Quarto's bundled typst compiler then
  compiles to PDF as a distinct final step.
- docx has the sharp edge: pandoc tags text with a style *name*
  (`Hyperlink`, `Table`, `Strong`, ...) and leaves formatting up to
  whatever `reference-doc` defines for that name. A name Word lists as
  available but never defines (a "latent" style) silently renders as plain
  text — no error, no warning. That's exactly what was wrong with
  hyperlinks in this template until it was fixed alongside this notebook —
  see the worked example below.
:::

# Why this notebook exists

Everything else under `supplementary/` is a minimal reproducible example —
render, look at the file, report what happened
([tables-mre.qmd](tables-mre.qmd), [images-mre.qmd](images-mre.qmd)). This
notebook explains a piece of *mechanism* instead: one that's easy to work
around without understanding, until it breaks in a way that only makes
sense once you know how the pieces fit together. It also doubles as the
writeup for a real bug found and fixed while writing it (the docx
hyperlink styling issue), a direct illustration of the mechanism.

# The pipeline, in one diagram

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-pipeline-overview
%%| fig-cap: "One .qmd, one knitr execution, many pandoc writers"
flowchart TD
    A[".qmd source<br/>prose + R code chunks"] --> B["knitr executes R chunks<br/>(cached by execute: freeze)"]
    B --> C["intermediate markdown<br/>chunk output already substituted in"]
    C --> D["Quarto Lua filters walk the AST<br/>e.g. crossref numbering,<br/>this project's flextable-qmd filter"]
    D --> E{"pandoc — one writer<br/>per target format"}
    E -->|html writer| F["manuscript.html"]
    E -->|docx writer| G["OOXML docx<br/>styled via reference-doc"]
    E -->|typst writer| H["manuscript.typ<br/>(Typst markup, not a PDF yet)"]
    H --> I["Quarto's bundled typst compiler"]
    I --> J["manuscript.pdf"]
```

Four stages, each confirmed by direct testing in this project rather than
assumed from documentation:

## 1. Compute — knitr runs once, not once per format

For an R-engine `.qmd` (the default in this `website`-type project — see
[CLAUDE.md](../../CLAUDE.md)'s "Core architectural decision"), knitr
executes every code chunk and produces **one** intermediate markdown
document with computed output already inlined — figures rendered to image
files and referenced, table objects printed, inline R expressions
evaluated. This happens once per `.qmd`, before pandoc sees anything, and
before Quarto hands off to three different writers.

This is exactly what `execute: freeze` controls
(`manuscript/_quarto.yml`): a cache over *this* stage specifically — skip
re-running knitr, reuse the last intermediate markdown. It has nothing to
do with pandoc or the downstream formats, which is why `freeze: true` can
serve stale *content* indefinitely (a real regression hit earlier in this
project, see CLAUDE.md's "Related, separate gotcha") while having zero
effect on a docx styling problem — that lives entirely in stage 3, below.

## 2. Filter — Lua filters walk the pandoc AST before conversion

Before any format-specific writer runs, Quarto passes the document through
Lua filters that operate on pandoc's internal document representation (its
abstract syntax tree, not text). Two are already active in this project's
`manuscript/_quarto.yml`:

- Quarto's own crossref filter, turning `@fig-example` / `@tbl-example` /
  `@eq-poisson` into numbered, format-appropriate links (`Figure 1`,
  `Table 1`, ...) — matters for the hyperlink styling bug below, since
  crossrefs render as real hyperlinks in docx, not plain text with a number.
- This project's own `filters: [flextable-qmd]` line, which makes the
  flextable-based tables in [tables-mre.qmd](tables-mre.qmd) work
  identically across formats.

## 3. Convert — pandoc, one writer per format, docx via named styles

This is the stage where html, docx, and typst genuinely diverge, and
diverge *differently* than you'd guess:

- **html**: pandoc writes HTML tags directly (`<a>`, `<table>`, ...) and
  the browser's own default styling (plus `styles/styles.css`) does the
  rest. A hyperlink is blue and underlined for free — just what browsers
  do with `<a>` tags, no extra work from pandoc or this project.
- **docx**: pandoc does **not** hand-craft formatting into the OOXML runs
  it writes. Instead it tags spans of text with a **style reference** —
  `<w:rStyle w:val="Hyperlink"/>` for link text, similarly for `Strong`,
  `Table`, and others already documented in CLAUDE.md — and leaves the
  actual color/weight/border/etc. to whatever `reference-doc` supplies for
  a style with that exact name. Same relationship as an HTML
  `class="..."` attribute and an external CSS file: the tag alone renders
  nothing.
- **typst**: pandoc writes **Typst markup** (`manuscript.typ`) — a distinct
  intermediate artifact, confirmed directly in a render log:
  `[typst]: Compiling manuscript.typ to manuscript.pdf...DONE`. This is
  pandoc's typst *writer*, functionally the same as its docx or html
  writer, just targeting a different output language.

## 4. Compile — only typst has this extra step

html and docx are pandoc's final output — nothing further happens to them.
typst differs: pandoc's output (`manuscript.typ`) is Typst source code, and
Quarto's bundled Typst compiler (no separate system install needed —
confirmed in CLAUDE.md) compiles *that* into `manuscript.pdf`. A
typst-stage failure is a compile error in generated Typst markup, not a
pandoc conversion error — also why one bad image format can hard-fail
typst specifically while html/docx sail through unaffected (documented in
[images-mre.qmd](images-mre.qmd)'s TIFF section): the failure happens one
stage later than for the other two formats.

# Worked example: the docx hyperlink styling gap

This project hit a real instance of the stage-3 failure mode above:
hyperlinks rendered as working links in docx (clicking them navigated
correctly) but displayed as plain black, non-underlined text — nothing
visually marked them as links, unlike html where this is automatic.

## Confirming the mechanism, not guessing at it

Generating a one-line test document and inspecting the raw OOXML pandoc
produced showed exactly what stage 3 predicts:

```xml
<w:hyperlink r:id="rId20">
  <w:r>
    <w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="Hyperlink" /></w:rPr>
    <w:t xml:space="preserve">link to example</w:t>
  </w:r>
</w:hyperlink>
```

Pandoc *is* tagging the run with `w:rStyle val="Hyperlink"` unconditionally
— never the problem. Checking both `styles/db-space-line-n.docx` and
`styles/standard.docx` (this project's two reference docs) for an actual
definition of that style turned up nothing:

```xml
<!-- word/styles.xml in both reference docs had: -->
<w:lsdException w:name="Hyperlink" w:semiHidden="1" w:unhideWhenUsed="1"/>
<!--
  i.e. Word knows "Hyperlink" as a style NAME it could show in the
  style gallery, but no <w:style w:styleId="Hyperlink"> block anywhere
  actually defines what it looks like.
-->
```

That `<w:lsdException>` entry is a **latent style declaration** — Word's
way of saying "this name is reserved/known, offer it in the UI if asked,"
different from a style *definition*
(`<w:style w:type="character" w:styleId="Hyperlink">...<w:rPr>...`). Pandoc
references the name; nothing in either reference doc backed it with actual
formatting. Same failure shape CLAUDE.md already documents for the
`Table` style — "missing style falls back to a generic default, Word does
not warn you" — independently rediscovered here for `Hyperlink`.

## The fix

A character style definition was added to both reference docs, based on
Word's own built-in default for this exact style name:

```xml
<w:style w:type="character" w:styleId="Hyperlink">
  <w:name w:val="Hyperlink"/>
  <w:basedOn w:val="DefaultParagraphFont"/>
  <w:uiPriority w:val="99"/>
  <w:unhideWhenUsed/>
  <w:rPr>
    <w:color w:val="0563C1"/>
    <w:u w:val="single"/>
  </w:rPr>
</w:style>
```

Confirmed end-to-end, not just in the reference doc alone: a full
`targets::tar_make()` rebuild, followed by extracting
`manuscript/output/manuscript.docx`'s own `word/document.xml` and
`word/styles.xml`, showed **six** hyperlink runs in the real manuscript —
cross-references to an equation, a section, a figure, and a table, plus a
citation link — all tagged `w:rStyle val="Hyperlink"`, with the
now-present style definition sitting in the output docx's own
`styles.xml`. Every one was previously unstyled in Word, not just plain
external URLs; Quarto's crossref filter (stage 2) renders cross-references
as real docx hyperlinks, so the bug silently affected in-document
navigation too, not only links to outside URLs.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-hyperlink-sequence
%%| fig-cap: "Why an undefined style renders silently instead of erroring"
sequenceDiagram
    participant MD as intermediate markdown/AST
    participant PW as pandoc docx writer
    participant RD as reference-doc styles.xml
    participant W as Word

    MD->>PW: "[link text](url)" / a crossref
    PW->>PW: wrap the run in w:rStyle val="Hyperlink"
    PW->>RD: look up styleId="Hyperlink"
    alt style actually defined
        RD-->>PW: color + underline copied into output docx
        PW->>W: styled blue, underlined text
    else style only latent (name known, never defined)
        RD-->>PW: nothing to copy - no error raised
        PW->>W: falls back to Normal - no color, no warning
    end
```

## The general recipe

Any "it renders, but looks wrong specifically in Word, not in html"
symptom is worth checking against this same mechanism before suspecting
anything more exotic:

1. Generate a minimal doc containing the construct in question and convert
   it with pandoc directly (bypassing Quarto/targets, just to isolate the
   stage), then extract `word/document.xml` and look for the `w:rStyle` /
   `w:pStyle` name pandoc chose.
2. Check whether that exact style name is *defined* (has real `<w:rPr>` /
   `<w:pPr>` content), not just *listed*, in whichever `reference-doc` is
   configured in `manuscript/_quarto.yml`.
3. If it's missing or only latent, add a real `<w:style>` block for that
   name — matching Word's own built-in default is usually the least
   surprising choice, as done above for `Hyperlink`.

CLAUDE.md's "The Word reference doc" section lists the other style names
pandoc is already confirmed to reference this way (`Table`,
`TableCaption`, `BodyText`, `Compact`, ...) — the same check is worth
running against any of those if a new formatting gap turns up.

# References
